sentences.text: OK.
That was in Sosnowiec.
This was the last ghetto before they made judenrein.
This means to clean up the Jews.
And we all had to go out on a certain big place, of course.
The young people were on one side, the older one on the other side, mothers and children on their arms, little children next to them holding their mom.
Separations and separations and screaming and crying and prayers, always prayers.
And when I looked at-- I looked it up all the way because I knew my parents I won't find anymore.
So I was concentrating who else is not here or I can replace and do something for them.
And I saw a older men and a mother and a little child on her arm and a German guard start-- start to separate them.
And the father stretched his hand, and he said, give me the child.
I know where I'm going.
And I'm going to take him with me.
You will survive.
And several times I tried to replace somebody who was holding a child or the father or the mother.
It didn't work.
And after the selection-- this took days, the selection took days-- they took me and my sister to a camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one camp I was a short time.
This was--
By train.
We got there by train, separated.
They told us to keep everything what we have.
I had my suitcase from home.
And I wound up in Ottmuth.
And this is a city somewhere in Germany.
I don't remember where in.
And again, I cannot call this lucky because I don't call lucky being alive at all, but I wasn't punished like others.
The two camps that I went to were-- to compare-- like beautiful places to be according to the others, what my friends, my husband included, went through.
The ride on the train was-- was pretty bad because we were stuffed in like animals, without water, without anything, till we arrived.
This took a couple hours, maybe four or five hours, something like that.
On the train, I remember people wanted to jump out the windows, to open, but they couldn't.
And screaming and crying, where's my child?
Where's my father?
Where's my mother?
Little ones, older ones, we all were 15 or 16 or were 14 years old.
One girl, 12-year-old from my city.
And that's all that I remember, crying and screaming.
And we came to Ottmuth.
And I got a job in a factory.
When the train opened, the Germans with dogs and [SIGHS] the pistols and all the guns, what they had in front, and screaming-- unnecessary screaming but everybody followed anyway.
And threatening, and we should stay straight in line and just obey every little thing what they said.
Some people, they couldn't even understand what they were saying.
But we tried to help each other, the ones who knew each other.
We stayed together.
My sister was, at that time, still in ghetto.
Yeah, she was-- I met her in another camp.
And when we came to Ottmuth, they took us, of course, to a-- a concentration camp, which was a mild concentration camp.
What I mean by that is like they didn't cut their hairs.
I could wear whatever I had, my own clothes.
And we had a little bit of water every day to keep ourselves clean as much as we could.
The facilities wasn't that bad.
The breakfast-- well, this was a first time breakfast in a camp-- was a poor-- very poor breakfast because when they gave you a little bit of soup was [INAUDIBLE] and a slice of bread.
But to compare later on in life in camps, [? is all this-- ?] I remember this that's a good breakfast.
Then we all went to the factory.
And I had the job in the factory where I put together the right parts of the upper shoes, the leather, the right and the left in order, to make a pair of shoe.
I was very handy.
And I was fast and very particular in working.
So I happened to have a master who was very good to me, the one who delivered-- took away from me the work.
And he brought me every day a sandwich.
He didn't say anything.
But when I arrived there was a sandwich on my plate where I was sitting and working.
I didn't say, of course, I ate it.
And every day almost I took some parts, and he put them together and he made shoes.
It wasn't that strict.
That's why I could do it.
And the camp wasn't that such a strict camp either.
Were men and women together.
Again by train, even worse.
Because it was later on.
In the beginning it probably was a little bit milder.
Yeah.
People were fainting.
Some people died in the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we more-- more treated like really from beasts-- beasts treating animals.
And then Ludwigsdorf was not a great camp, not great for me.
I have to again repeat what my husband is telling me about his camp and what I heard about others, including Auschwitz in particular, and so and so.
Again, Ludwigsdorf was fair.
Yeah.
I could wear my clothes, no clothes from a camp.
Cutting hair I saw a few times or beating up by punishment only.
Of course, what was the punishment?
Stealing a potato or bringing in something-- something from the outside.
No.
With--
Spit.
With spit.
My shoes were always clean.
I tried to do my best wherever I could and to help myself.
I did-- didn't drink the water before I washed myself.
And she picked me up hundreds of girls to be her maid.
So working for her-- I didn't know what this means, hunger.
And-- sort of-- I had better than the others.
What it was?
OK.
She had, first of all, in the morning when I came in, I put her white collar on the uniform, pressed it for her, kept everything just so when she was going out to the appel.
She had a little dog and to keep up the dog, feed him around the house, not hard work, but just particular work where she likes-- she liked it.
And again, I was going on and on for a while till I started to do the wrong thing.
She had vodka in her closet.
Vodka was a drink what my father used to have Shabbat-- after Shabbat.
And he always took a sip, and he always said to me, you just-- on the tongue you can taste it.
And I don't know how I did.
I took out that vodka.
And I said, oh my God.
My father always said, taste it.
Because always I went back to things to see my father again and again and not thinking about the consequences.
And when she came in she smelled it, talking to me.
This I didn't know at that time, that you can find out the easy way without even checking the bottle.
And she said, I cannot keep you anymore here.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they transferred me to a kitchen, which was again not too bad, working with the kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was stealing.
I couldn't help not stealing, stealing and bringing food.
I was working with a girlfriend who kept up her religion the way how I was before the war.
During the war, I [INAUDIBLE] a little but.
I didn't go on with the [NON-ENGLISH].
Did my prayers.
But she did more than I did because she had her father, and she knew where her father is.
And she said, every day I'm going to pray three times a day that my father survives.
He did survive.
And she used to say, I'll help you.
I'm going to give you every day a bread.
And you take that bread to your room, hide it.
And the bread [INAUDIBLE] the day before take it out.
And we're going to bring this to the [GERMAN], the-- where the sick people were.
And we going to take a few slices with us and see who is really hungry to give them.
And that's what I did a while with help from my girlfriend.
We were throwing over the fence slices of bread.
We did a lot of things what we shouldn't do.
And later on, they didn't want any more Jewish-- they selected the Jewish girls from the-- the Christian kitchen.
And they took them in-- in the Jewish kitchen only.
Over there I did not work.
They took me to a factory where they made ammunition.
And this was very, very bad.
There were three colors-- yellow, gray, and a dark, dark gray, almost like black.
I was in the dark gray.
My skin, everything was so dark, like from a different race.
The only thing what we got-- that I remember was nice-- they gave us a glass of milk during the work.
Otherwise we probably wouldn't survive.
And I was working with that ammunition till liberation.
I was weighing on a scale.
I remember there was-- in front of me was a screen.
And I was sitting beside that screen.
And there was a small, tiny little scale.
And I was weighing the powder.
And if there was a tiny, tiny little bit more-- explode.
So this had to be exactly.
And there were chosen people for that.
And I was the one to be chosen.
And I was working there until the end.
Eight hours.
Night shift and day shift.
In the day in the-- in the camp.
And the-- and when they had day shift, we slept overnight.
Otherwise we slept during the day.
During the day, you couldn't sleep because otherwise they-- they didn't work on the same shift.
They shared the room.
But we went through.
I made friends, yes.
I made friends with the ones-- like the one who was sharing with me, the stealing sharing and the giving out the bread to the ones who need it more than we did.
She remains a very, very close friend.
She still lives in Florida.
I remember when she survived with her father came to camp.
And she survived with her brother.
Then I have several more friends from my own town who are here.
And of course my sister.
My sister was in same camp in Ludwigsdorf.
Yes.
Yeah, I saw her every day.
Every day.
Because she was working in the same ammunition place.
Oh, I remember when the men-- when we came there were still the men at the other side.
This I remember-- being shot, caught.
I remember certain punishments.
This punishment I also won't forget in my life.
When we came in, there were men to that camp.
There were still men at the other side.
There were a fountain in the middle of the camp.
And there was a young man, at that time probably was 14 , 15, he was chained under the fountain.
And the-- that water was-- Yes, I knew him very well from back home.
My sister survived with me.
And she went with a girlfriend, also from our hometown, to Vienna, to look up for her brother.
She found out through the Red Cross that her brother was alive.
She needed companionship.
So my sister said, I'll go [? with you. ?] You stay here in Ludwigsdorf.
Remain in Ludwigsdorf.
For months, I was still at the same camp.
I didn't go out from camp.
I could go out.
I could establish my own life, see what I want to do.
But I wasn't ready for anything.
I thought the camp and if I have water enough to keep myself clean and food, probably could stay there as long as they are going to let me.
And we were liberated by the Russians.
8 Of May, 1945.
What happened?
I remember, all of a sudden, we heard 'come out, come out,' you know, in Russian.
And already were people from other camps close around who were, a few hours before, they were liberated, talking in Jewish mainly.
Come out.
Don't be afraid anymore.
The Germans are gone.
We are liberated.
We are free.
I felt like-- not happy, not happy, not want it to happen without thinking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why's it happen?
And what's happened?
It took me a few days, not only me my friends-- we were surrounded in a group always.
I was never alone.
And we were thinking, let's see what's gonna happen now.
Where we belong, to who we belong, and what we want to do?
And the main question, why has it happen?
What did?
To who do you ask that question?
I was thankful to God for only one thing, that I had my sister.
And I looked at her and I said, well, that's what God wanted.
And it took me, really, weeks.
Not-- not that I couldn't put myself physically together, mentally.
Physically, I looked good compared to the others who I saw at the door from other camps.
And I'm talking about months later.
But mentally, I couldn't put myself together and think about what I want to do.
So the first think in my mind was I'll go back home.
And I'll go to the cemetery where I knew my grandfather was buried in the-- during the war.
No, my grandfather was buried shortly before the war-- the war broke out.
That's right, the end of '38.
My grandmother, as soon the war broke out.
My grandmother, I remember, they saying she died from agony knowing that she lost already in the war, when the war broke out she lost [BACKGROUND NOISE] already grandsons-- two grandsons.
And she died of that.
And I did so.
I went back to Poland.
I closed my eyes when I passed by my house where I used to live.
I didn't go out.
And I didn't want to see anybody but the cemetery.
I went to the cemetery.
I couldn't find the grave of my grandmother.
But my grandfather was next to the rabbi from our town.
And that grave was not only not touched, it was like kept up, like you would have done it now.
And I took a picture from the grave, not me, a Pole, and he gave me the picture.
And I came back.
OK.
